content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 9, 2001

Josephine Poole
815 Morningside Circle
Gilroy, CA 95020-3202

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-01-198

Dear Ms. Poole:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since your question does not pertain to a specific
upcoming governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance.²

QUESTION

Is a conflict of interest created based on the receipt of a "finder's fee" for
referring someone to another real estate agent within your firm?

CONCLUSION

No. While the source of the finder's fee is a source of income, absent a
governmental decision by the Public Guardian's office involving the source of the
finder's fee, there is no conflict of interest created by receiving the fee.

FACTS

You are currently, and have been employed by the Santa Clara County Public
Guardian's office for 13 years. Since November 1996, you have been in a position to
place your clients in many facilities which provide care. In July 2001, you went to look
at a new facility recently purchased by one of many owners of facilities (Amor). At that
time, you mentioned that you had a real estate license and worked part-time in the real
estate business. The owner said she would like you to help her locate apartments (not a

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by formal
written advice. (Government Code section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. section 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-01-198
Page No. 2

facility where a client would be placed for an investment. You informed her that you
believed it was a conflict of interest (or potentially, a conflict), and said you were not able
to help her. She then said she had a friend who lives in Canada and just sold a property
in Los Angeles and wanted a broker to assist him with a purchase in Santa Clara County.
You told her you did not work with investment properties, but would refer her friend to
an investment broker/sales agent within your office. A couple of weeks later, the owner
called you to provide the name of her friend and said that she had given her friend the
name and number of one other agent/broker in Santa Clara County. You gave the name
of the owner's friend to Bo Sepehr, who then made contact with the investor from
Canada. Subsequently, Bo Sepehr completed an agreement to pay you a percentage of
his commission if and when a real estate purchase is completed. You do not want to
accept a finder's fee if it is inappropriate. You indicated in a telephone conversation that
there is no connection between Bo Sepehr and your duties or position with the Public
Guardian's office. You also indicated that you are employed as a part-time real estate
sales agent for Century 21, a real estate brokerage.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials "should
perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial
interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (§ 81001(b).)

Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests
(§ 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted a material analysis for
deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given
governmental decision, which is applied here. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

1.     Public official.

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(§§ 87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(1).) "Public official" is defined as "every
member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency . . . ."
(§ 82048.)

A "local government agency" means a county, city or district of any kind,
including a school district, or any other local political subdivision, or any county board
commission (§ 82041.) As an employee of the Santa Clara County Public Guardian's
office, you are a "public official," for purposes of the Act (see §§ 82048, 82041), and the
conflict-of-interest rules apply to you.

File No. 1-01-198
Page No. 3

2. Will you be participating in a governmental decision?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions come into play only when a public
official makes, participates in making, or in some way attempts to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the official knows — or has
reason to know — that he or she has a financial interest. (§ 87100.) Commission
regulations describe in detail what constitutes making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision. (Regulations 18702.1, 18702.2, and 18702.3,
respectively.) You make, participate in making, or influence a governmental decision if
you participate in decisions regarding placement of individuals in various facilities.

3. Does the public official have economic interests?

Under section 87103 of the Act, there are six different types of economic
interests that may result in a conflict of interest for a public official:

A. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity where the
public official has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more in the business entity.
(§ 87103(a); Reg. 18703.1(a).)

B. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity where
the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management in the business entity. (§ 87103(d); Reg. 18703.1(b).)

C. A public official has an economic interest in any real property in which
the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more in fair
market value. (§ 87103(b); Reg. 18703.2.)

D. A public official has an economic interest in any person from whom
he/she has received income aggregating $500 within 12 months prior to the time
when the relevant governmental decision is made. (§ 87103(c); Reg. 18703.3.)

E. A public official has an economic interest in any donor of, or any
intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating $320 or more in
value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made. (§ 87103(e); Reg. 18703.4.)

F. A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal
finances. (§ 87103.) In particular, a governmental decision has a personal
financial effect on a public official if the decision will result in the personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate
family increasing or decreasing by an amount of $250 or more. (§ 87103; Reg.
18703.5; 18705.5.)

Your question concerns two types of economic interests recognized under
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. First, you have an economic interest
in any person from whom you have received income aggregating $500 within 12
months prior to the time when the relevant governmental decision is made.

File No. I-01-198
Page No. 4

(§ 87103(c), Reg. 18703.3.) Second, you have an economic interest in your
personal finances.

Addressing the "personal financial effects" rule, "[a] governmental decision will
have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will result in the personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family
increasing or decreasing." (Regulation 18703.5.) However, we can eliminate this basis
for disqualification at the outset. The request for advice does not indicate that your
personal finances will be affected in any way by placement decisions that you make.

Your request for advice indicates that you have not, and will not receive any
income from any source involved in any governmental decision currently pending before
you. Nevertheless, regulation 18703.3 provides guidelines for the attribution of
commission income for conflict-of-interest purposes. Regulation 18703.3 provides:

"(3) The sources of commission income in a specific sale or similar transaction
include for each of the following:

"(A) An insurance broker or agent:

¶. . .¶

"(B) A real estate broker:

¶. . .¶

"(C) A real estate agent:

¶. . .¶

"(ii) The person the agent represents in the transaction; and

"(iii) Any person who receives a finder's or other referral fee for referring
a party to the transaction to the broker, or who makes a referral pursuant to a contract
with the broker.

¶. . .¶

"(4) For purposes of determining whether disqualification is required under the
provisions of sections 87100 and 87103(c), the full gross value of any commission
income for a specific sale or similar transaction shall be attributed to each source of
income in that sale or transaction." (Reg. 18703.3(c).)

Thus, pursuant to Regulation 18703.3, you also have an economic interest in the
brokerage firm, the clients of the brokerage firm that you represent and anyone who
makes a referral pursuant to a contract with the broker, assuming you receive commission
income at or above the $500 threshold. Under your facts, the source of the promised
income about which you inquired is the other realtor, Bo Sepehr, and not Amor. Thus,
whether you receive the income or not, Amor will not be considered an economic
interest.³

Of course, you will continue to have a potential conflict of interest in any decision
that will have a material and foreseeable financial effect on your sources of income,

_____________
3 "Income" includes promised income. Regulation 18703.3(a) instructs that a public official's income
includes income which has been promised to the public official but not yet received by him or her.

File No. I-01-198
Page No. 5

Century 21 and the other real estate agent. However, since you have not described any
specific decision that may impact these sources we can only give you this general
description of the conflict of interest analysis applied to them.

4.     Is your economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the decision?

Regulation 18704.1(a) provides:

"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an
official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made
by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's
agency. A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with,
the subject person."

Thus, for example, if either Bo Sepehr or your Century 21 brokerage were to have
initiated proceedings before the Public Guardian's office that employs you, your
economic interest would be directly involved in a governmental decision.

5. & 6. Choosing which materiality standards to apply in deciding if there will be a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect.

This two-step process involves finding and applying the applicable materiality
standard set forth in Commission regulations. (Regulation 18700(b)(5), Regulation
18705, et seq.) After finding the applicable materiality standard, you must then decide in
step six whether it is reasonably foreseeable that this standard will be met or exceeded.
(Regulation 18700(b)(6).)

Where a source of income is a business entity and that business entity is indirectly
involved in a governmental decision, the Commission analyzes this interest under
Regulation 18705.1(c). For example, the source of income to you is the brokerage firm
that employs you, as well as clients that you represent and anyone who makes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker. Assuming the business entity is not publicly
traded and it is of relatively small financial size, Regulation 18705.1(c)(4) provides that
"the financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity is material if it is
reasonably foreseeable that:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or

File No. I-01-198
Page No. 6

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."

If the source of income is an individual, such as Bo Sepehr, the effect will be
material if "[t]he decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or other
tangible or intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000 or more, or
the decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a manner that is
considered material under regulation 18705.2(b)." (Regulation 18705.3 (b)(3)(A-B)).

A material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if it is "substantially likely"
to occur. (Regulation 18706.) A material financial effect need not be a certainty, but it
must be more than a mere possibility. As used here, "reasonably foreseeable" means
"substantially likely." (Regulation 18706; (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198).
Whether the financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at
the time the decision is made is highly situation-specific. On the other hand, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (Ibid.)

7. & 8. Exceptions

Step seven is an exception that applies where the reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on the official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally, and step eight is an exception that applies when the official
is legally required to participate in the decision.


If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,


Luisa Menchaca,
General Counsel

By: Jody Feldman, Staff Counsel
Legal Division

sections.conclusion:
No. While the source of the finder's fee is a source of income, absent a
governmental decision by the Public Guardian's office involving the source of the
finder's fee, there is no conflict of interest created by receiving the fee.

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials "should
perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial
interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." ($ 81001(b).)
Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public oflicial from making,

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests
($ 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commissionhas adopted a standard analysis for
deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given
governmental decision, which is applied here. (Regulation 18700(bX1)-(g) )

A "local government agency" means a county, city or district of any kind,

commission ($ 82041.) As an employee of the Santa Clara County Public Guardian's

or has

he or she has a financial interest. ($ 87100.) Commission

respectively ) You make, participate in making, or influence a governmental decision if
you participate in decisions regarding placement of individuals in various facilities.
3. 
Does the public official have economic interests?
Under section 87103 of the Act, there are six different types of economic
interests that may result in a conflict of interest for a public official:
A. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity where the
public official has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more in the business entity

the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any

C. A public official has an economic interest in any real property in which
the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more in fair

D. A public official has an economic interest in any person from whom
helshe has received income aggregating $500 within 12 months prior to the time
when the relevant governmental decision is made. (g 87103(c); Reg. 18703.3.)
E. A public official has an economic interest in any donor of, or any
intermediary or agent for a donor of a gift or gifts aggregatrng $320 or more in
value provided to, received by, or promised to the public offlrcial within IZ
months prior to the time when the decision is made. (g 87103(e); Reg. 18703.4.)

finances. ($ 87103.) In particular, a governmental decision has a personal

expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate
family increasing or decreasing by an amount of $250 or more. ($ 87103, Reg
18703 5; 18705 5 )
Your question concerns two types of economic interests recognized under
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. First, you have an economic interest
in any person from whom you have received income aggregattng $500 within 12
months prior to the time when the relevant governmental decision is made.
($ 87103(c); Reg 18703.3.) Second, you have an economic interest in your
personal finances.
Addressing the "personal financial effects" rule, "[a] governmental decision will
have an effect on this economic interest if the decision will result in the personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family
increasing or decreasing." (Regulation 18703.5.) However, we can eliminate this basis
for disqualification at the outset. The request for advice does not indicate that your

Century 2I andthe other real estate agent. However, since you have not described any

4. 
Is your economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
Regulation 187 04.1 (a) provides:
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an
official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:

by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's
agency. A person is the subject ofa proceeding ifa decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with,
the subject person."
Thus, for example, if either Bo Sepehr or your Century 21 brokerage were to have
initiated proceedings before the Public Guardian's offrce that employs you, your
economic interest would be directly involved in a governmental decision.
5. & 6. Choosing which materiality standards to apply in deciding if there will be a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect.
This two-step process involves finding and applying the applicable materiality
standard set forth in Commission regulations. (Regulation 18700(b)(5), Regulation
18705, et seq.) After finding the applicable materiality standard, you must then decide in
step six whether it is reasonably foreseeable that this standard will be met or exceeded.
(Regulation 18700(bX6) )
Where a source of income is a business entity and that business entity is indirectly
involved in a governmental decision, the Commission analyzes this interest under
Regulation 18705.1(c). For example, the source of income to you is the brokerage firm
that employs you, as well as clients that you represent and anyone who makes a referral
pursuant to a contract with the broker. Assuming the business entity is not publicly
traded and it is of relatively small financial size, Regulation 18705 1(c)(a) provides that
"the financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity is material if it is
reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."
If the source of income is an individual, such as Bo Sepehr, the effect will be
material if "[t]he decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or other
tangible orintangible assets orliabilities (otherthanrealproperty) by $1,000 ormore, or
the decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a manner that is
considered material under regulation 18705.2(b)." (Regulation I8705.3 (b)(3XA-B)
A material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if it is "substantially likely"
to occur. (Regulation 18706.) A material financial effect need not be a certainty, but it
must be more than a mere possibility As used here, "reasonably foreseeable" means
"substantially likely." (Regulation 18706; (In re Thorner (1975) 1 Fppc ops. 198).
Whether the financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at
the time the decision is made is highly situation-specific. On the other hand, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (fbid.)
7. & 8. Exceptions
Step seven is an exception that applies where the reasonably foreseeable and
material financial effect on the official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally, and step eight is an exception that applies when the official
is legally required to participate in the decision.

embedding.qa_text:
CONCLUSION: No. While the source of the finder's fee is a source of income, absent a
governmental decision by the Public Guardian's office involving the source of the
finder's fee, there is no conflict of interest created by receiving the fee.
